questions.question: What is the name of the file CMake looks for in each directory to define the build?
questions.explanation: CMake reads CMakeLists.txt files in each directory. This is the only filename CMake recognizes as a build definition file.

questions.question: What are the three phases of CMake's workflow in correct order?
questions.explanation: CMake first configures (reads CMakeLists.txt and sets variables), then generates native build system files, then builds using that native system.

questions.question: CMake is a build system that directly compiles source code into executables.
questions.explanation: False. CMake is a build system generator — it generates native build files (Makefiles, Ninja files, VS projects) which then perform the actual compilation.

questions.question: Which command sets the minimum required CMake version for a project?
questions.explanation: cmake_minimum_required(VERSION x.y) must be the first command in the top-level CMakeLists.txt. It sets the minimum version and implicitly sets policies.

questions.question: In CMake's scripting language, how do you dereference a variable named MY_VAR?
questions.explanation: ${MY_VAR} is the variable reference syntax in CMake. The @MY_VAR@ syntax is only used in configure_file() template substitution.

questions.question: In CMake, all variables are strings — even lists are stored as semicolon-separated strings.
questions.explanation: True. CMake has no native list type. Lists are strings with semicolons as separators. The list() command provides operations on these semicolon-separated strings.

questions.question: Which CMake command creates an executable target from source files?
questions.explanation: add_executable(target_name source1.cpp source2.cpp) creates an executable target. add_library() creates libraries, and target_sources() adds sources to existing targets.

questions.question: What type of build does this CMake command configure?
questions.explanation: -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release sets an optimized release build (typically -O2 or -O3 flags).

questions.question: Which keyword in add_library() creates a library that is compiled into the final executable (not a separate .so/.dll)?
questions.explanation: STATIC libraries (.a/.lib) are archives of object files linked directly into the executable. SHARED creates dynamic libraries (.so/.dll).

questions.question: What is the difference between a SHARED and a MODULE library in CMake?
options: MODULE cannot be linked against, only loaded at runtime (dlopen)
SHARED is deprecated in favor of MODULE
questions.explanation: MODULE libraries are designed to be loaded at runtime via dlopen()/LoadLibrary() (like plugins). They cannot be used with target_link_libraries(). SHARED libraries can be both linked and loaded at runtime.

questions.question: In target_link_libraries(myapp PUBLIC mylib), what does PUBLIC mean?
options: mylib is only linked, not propagated
mylib is propagated to anything that links against myapp
questions.explanation: PUBLIC means the dependency is both used by myapp AND propagated transitively to consumers of myapp. PRIVATE means used only by myapp. INTERFACE means only propagated to consumers.

questions.question: Which visibility keyword should you use for a dependency that your library uses internally but does not expose in its public headers?
questions.explanation: PRIVATE dependencies are used to build the target but not propagated to consumers. Since the dependency doesn't appear in public headers, consumers don't need it.

questions.question: What is an INTERFACE library in CMake used for?
options: A library with no source files — only propagates usage requirements (includes, defines, flags)
A library that implements an abstract interface class
A dynamic library loaded at runtime
A library only available on the build machine
questions.explanation: INTERFACE libraries have no compiled output. They carry usage requirements (include dirs, compile definitions, link flags) that are transitively propagated to anything that links against them. Common for header-only libraries.

questions.question: The CMake variable _____ sets the C++ standard version (e.g., 17, 20, 23) for a project.
questions.explanation: CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD (or the target property CXX_STANDARD) sets which C++ standard to use. Common values: 11, 14, 17, 20, 23.

questions.question: What does target_compile_features(mylib PUBLIC cxx_std_17) do compared to setting CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD?
options: compile_features is per-target and propagates transitively; CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD is a global default
CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD cannot be used with targets
questions.explanation: target_compile_features() sets the requirement per-target and propagates via PUBLIC/PRIVATE/INTERFACE. CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD sets a global default. The per-target approach is more modern and precise.

questions.question: What is the purpose of generator expressions in CMake (e.g., $<CONFIG:Release>)?
options: They generate random values for testing
They are evaluated at generate-time, allowing conditional logic based on build config, platform, or compiler
questions.explanation: Generator expressions are evaluated during build system generation (not during configure). They allow conditional content based on build type, target properties, compiler, platform, etc.

questions.question: What flags does this generator expression add in a Debug build?
questions.explanation: The $<$<CONFIG:Debug>:...> genex evaluates to the content only when building in Debug mode. In Release, it expands to nothing.

questions.question: Which CMake module and command detect the host operating system at configure time?
questions.explanation: CMAKE_SYSTEM_NAME is automatically set by CMake to 'Linux', 'Windows', 'Darwin', etc. You can check it with if(CMAKE_SYSTEM_NAME STREQUAL "Linux").

questions.question: What does find_package(Boost REQUIRED COMPONENTS filesystem system) do?
options: Searches for a pre-installed Boost and fails if not found, importing filesystem and system components
questions.explanation: find_package() searches for already-installed packages using Find modules or config files. REQUIRED means CMake errors if not found. COMPONENTS specifies which parts are needed.

questions.question: What is the difference between a Find module (FindXxx.cmake) and a config-file package (XxxConfig.cmake)?
options: Find modules are written by CMake/users to locate packages; config files are provided by the package itself
Find modules only work on Linux
questions.explanation: Find modules are CMake scripts that search for packages heuristically. Config files are exported by the package during its own install, providing exact targets and paths. Config files are preferred when available.

questions.question: Which CMake module downloads and integrates third-party dependencies at configure time?
questions.explanation: FetchContent (available since CMake 3.11) downloads dependencies during configure and makes them part of your build. ExternalProject downloads at build time (not configure time).

questions.question: What is the key difference between FetchContent and ExternalProject in CMake?
options: FetchContent integrates at configure-time (targets available immediately); ExternalProject builds at build-time (targets not available during configure)
questions.explanation: FetchContent downloads and configures during CMake's configure step, so targets are immediately usable. ExternalProject defers everything to build time, meaning you can't use target_link_libraries() with its outputs directly.

questions.question: In a CMake superbuild pattern, what module is typically used for each dependency?
questions.explanation: The superbuild pattern uses ExternalProject_Add() for each dependency, building them in isolation with their own configure/build/install steps, then uses find_package() in the main project to consume the installed results.

questions.question: Which CMake command registers a test so it can be run by ctest?
questions.explanation: add_test(NAME test_name COMMAND executable) registers a test. But enable_testing() must be called first in the top-level CMakeLists.txt to enable CTest integration.

questions.question: enable_testing() must be called before any add_test() commands, and it must be in the top-level CMakeLists.txt.
questions.explanation: True. enable_testing() must be called in the top-level CMakeLists.txt (not just a subdirectory) for ctest to discover tests. All add_test() calls must come after it.

questions.question: When integrating GoogleTest with CMake, which command auto-discovers test cases from a GTest executable?
questions.explanation: gtest_discover_tests() (from GoogleTest module) runs the test executable with --gtest_list_tests at CTest time to discover all TEST() and TEST_F() cases automatically. gtest_add_tests() is an older, less reliable approach.

questions.question: What does configure_file(config.h.in config.h) do?
options: Substitutes @VAR@ and ${VAR} placeholders with CMake variable values and writes the output
questions.explanation: configure_file() reads an input template, replaces @VAR@ (and optionally ${VAR}) with current CMake variable values, and writes the result to the output path. Used to embed version numbers, paths, and feature flags into source code.

questions.question: Which approach generates source code at build time (not configure time) in CMake?
questions.explanation: add_custom_command(OUTPUT ...) runs at build time when the output is needed. file(GENERATE) also generates at generation time. configure_file() runs at configure time only. Both B and C can generate source at build/generate time.

questions.question: In a well-structured CMake project, where should you place the top-level project() command?
options: Only in the root CMakeLists.txt, after cmake_minimum_required()
questions.explanation: The project() command should appear in the root CMakeLists.txt immediately after cmake_minimum_required(). It sets the project name, version, languages, and establishes key variables like PROJECT_SOURCE_DIR.

questions.question: Modern CMake best practice recommends using target_include_directories() over the global include_directories() command.
questions.explanation: True. target_include_directories() is target-scoped and supports visibility specifiers (PUBLIC/PRIVATE/INTERFACE). The global include_directories() affects all targets and doesn't support transitive propagation.

questions.question: What CMake version first introduced experimental support for C++20 modules?
questions.explanation: CMake 3.28 introduced experimental support for C++20 modules with the CXX_MODULE_STD and FILE_SET features. Earlier versions had no module support.

questions.question: How do you integrate clang-tidy with a CMake build?
options: It's not possible without external scripts
questions.explanation: Setting CMAKE_CXX_CLANG_TIDY (or the target property CXX_CLANG_TIDY) tells CMake to run clang-tidy alongside the compiler for every source file. This is the simplest integration.

questions.question: Which tool is most commonly integrated with CMake for generating API documentation from C++ comments?
questions.explanation: Doxygen is the de facto standard for C/C++ API documentation. CMake provides FindDoxygen module and doxygen_add_docs() command for seamless integration.

questions.question: What does the GNUInstallDirs module provide?
options: Standard installation directory variables (CMAKE_INSTALL_BINDIR, CMAKE_INSTALL_LIBDIR, etc.)
questions.explanation: GNUInstallDirs defines standard install directories following the GNU conventions (bin/, lib/, include/, share/, etc.) so your install() commands are portable across Linux distributions.

questions.question: What does CPack do in a CMake project?
options: Creates distributable packages (DEB, RPM, ZIP, NSIS installers, etc.)
questions.explanation: CPack is CMake's packaging tool. It takes the install() rules and creates platform-native packages: .deb for Debian, .rpm for Red Hat, .zip/.tar.gz archives, NSIS/WiX installers for Windows, .dmg for macOS.

questions.question: CMake presets are defined in a file named _____ at the project root.
questions.explanation: CMakePresets.json (and optionally CMakeUserPresets.json for local overrides) defines named configure/build/test presets. Introduced in CMake 3.19.

questions.question: How do you add Fortran or C source files alongside C++ in a CMake project?
options: Use add_executable() with mixed sources — CMake auto-detects languages
Enable languages in project() command: project(MyProject LANGUAGES CXX C Fortran)
Create separate projects for each language
Use custom build rules for non-C++ files
questions.explanation: The project() command's LANGUAGES argument tells CMake which compilers to find. You must list all languages used. CMake then routes each source file to the appropriate compiler based on extension.

questions.question: When cross-compiling with CMake, you specify the target platform via the _____ variable pointing to a toolchain file.
questions.explanation: CMAKE_TOOLCHAIN_FILE points to a .cmake file that sets the target system, compiler paths, and sysroot. It must be set before the first project() call (typically via -DCMAKE_TOOLCHAIN_FILE=...).

questions.question: CMAKE_TOOLCHAIN_FILE must be set before the project() command is called for cross-compilation to work correctly.
questions.explanation: True. The toolchain file is processed during the project() call when CMake detects compilers. If set after project(), the compilers are already configured for the host, not the target.

questions.question: Which CMake generator produces the fastest builds for most projects?
questions.explanation: Ninja is designed for speed — it has minimal overhead, excellent parallelism, and faster dependency checking than Make. It's the recommended generator for CI/CD and development on all platforms.

questions.question: How do you specify which generator CMake should use?
options: Pass -G "Generator Name" on the cmake command line
questions.explanation: The -G flag specifies the generator: cmake -G "Ninja" -S . -B build. You can also set the CMAKE_GENERATOR environment variable. The generator cannot be changed after initial configuration.

questions.question: What is CDash in the CMake ecosystem?
options: A web-based dashboard for collecting and displaying test/build results from CTest
questions.explanation: CDash is a web application that receives build, test, and coverage results submitted by CTest. It provides dashboards showing build health, test failures, and trends across platforms.

questions.question: Which technique helps achieve reproducible builds in CMake?
options: Setting CMAKE_BUILD_RPATH_USE_ORIGIN, pinning dependency versions, and using deterministic flags
questions.explanation: Reproducible builds require pinned versions (FetchContent GIT_TAG with full SHA), deterministic compiler flags (-ffile-prefix-map), fixed timestamps, and consistent tool versions.

questions.question: Which CMake feature dramatically speeds up rebuilds by caching compiled object files?
options: All of the above improve build performance
questions.explanation: All three improve build performance: ccache caches object files across builds, precompiled headers reduce header parsing, and unity builds combine source files to reduce compiler invocations.

questions.question: CMAKE_UNITY_BUILD combines multiple source files into single translation units to reduce build times.
questions.explanation: True. Unity builds (jumbo builds) concatenate multiple .cpp files into one, reducing compiler startup overhead and redundant header parsing. Enable with set(CMAKE_UNITY_BUILD ON) or per-target.

questions.question: What is the correct install() command to install a library target with its headers?
options: install(TARGETS mylib EXPORT mylib-targets ARCHIVE DESTINATION lib LIBRARY DESTINATION lib INCLUDES DESTINATION include)
questions.explanation: install(TARGETS ...) with EXPORT and destination specifiers properly installs the library binary and creates an export set. The INCLUDES DESTINATION sets the include path in the exported target.

questions.question: In a professional CMake project, what is the purpose of a namespace in exported targets (e.g., MyLib::core)?
options: Namespaces make typos in target names into hard errors (instead of silent -lMyLib linker flags) and clarify provenance
questions.explanation: Namespaced targets (Foo::Bar) ensure that if you misspell a target name, CMake gives a configure-time error instead of silently passing a -l flag to the linker. They also clarify which package provides which target.

questions.question: How do you run tests in parallel with CTest?
questions.explanation: ctest -j N runs up to N tests in parallel. You can also set the CTEST_PARALLEL_LEVEL environment variable or use --parallel as an alias for -j.

questions.question: What does the -S flag mean in 'cmake -S . -B build'?
questions.explanation: -S specifies the source directory (where the top-level CMakeLists.txt lives). -B specifies the build directory. This out-of-source build pattern keeps generated files separate from source.

questions.question: When porting a legacy Makefile project to CMake, what is the recommended first step?
options: Map existing targets (executables, libraries) to CMake add_executable/add_library, preserving the same source groupings
Convert all source files to a single executable
questions.explanation: The best approach is incremental: map each existing build artifact to a CMake target, keeping the same logical structure. This allows validation at each step and doesn't require understanding the entire build at once.
